question: In what year was Java first publicly released?
feedback: Java was released in 1995 by Sun Microsystems. It was designed from the start to be portable across platforms through its 'Write Once, Run Anywhere' model using the JVM.

question: What must the filename of a public Java class match?
correct: The class name, including capitalization
incorrect: Any name, as long as it ends in .java
The name of the package the class belongs to
The name of the main method inside the class
feedback: A public class named MyProgram must be saved as MyProgram.java — same spelling, same capitalization. The compiler enforces this rule and will refuse to compile a file where the name doesn't match.

question: Which symbol is required at the end of every Java statement?
feedback: Every Java statement ends with a semicolon (;). Missing one is a syntax error and the program will not compile. This is different from languages like Python that use indentation to delimit statements.

question: What does // do in Java?
correct: Starts a single-line comment that the compiler ignores
incorrect: Performs integer division
Marks the start of a code block
Declares a constant
feedback: // begins a single-line comment. Everything from // to the end of the line is ignored by the compiler. Use comments to explain why the code works, not just what it does.

question: What class is used to read keyboard input from the console in Java?
correct: Scanner
incorrect: Reader
Input
Keyboard
feedback: The Scanner class (from java.util) reads text input from System.in. Create it with: Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in); then call nextLine(), nextInt(), etc.

question: What is the result of 7 / 2 in Java when both operands are int?
feedback: When both operands are integers, Java performs integer division and truncates the decimal. 7 / 2 becomes 3 (not 3.5). To get a decimal result, at least one operand must be a double: 7.0 / 2 = 3.5.

question: What output does System.out.print("A"); System.out.print("B"); produce?
correct: AB
incorrect: A B

A
B

A
B
feedback: print() does not add a newline — the cursor stays on the same line. Both calls output on the same line with no space between them: AB. Use println() to add a newline after each value.

question: What does setVisible(true) do on a JFrame?
correct: Makes the window appear on the screen
incorrect: Creates a new window object in memory
Brings the window in front of all other applications
Sets the window background to visible (non-transparent)
feedback: JFrame objects start invisible. You must call setVisible(true) after configuring the frame to display it. Forgetting this call is a common beginner mistake — the program runs but nothing appears.

question: Which Swing component displays non-interactive text or an image?
correct: JLabel
incorrect: JButton
JTextField
JPanel
feedback: JLabel displays static text or an image that the user cannot edit. JButton is clickable. JTextField accepts user input. JPanel is a container for organizing other components.

question: What is the purpose of a JAR file?
correct: To package compiled .class files into a single archive for distribution or execution
incorrect: To store Java source code in compressed format
To replace the JVM on systems that do not support it
To document the public API of a Java library
feedback: A JAR (Java Archive) bundles .class files and resources into one file. A program can be run directly from a JAR with 'java -jar program.jar', or a library JAR can be included as a dependency.

question: What are curly braces {} used for in Java?
correct: To define the boundaries of a code block
incorrect: To wrap the arguments passed to a method
To declare an array of values
To mark the end of a statement
feedback: Curly braces group statements into blocks: class bodies, method bodies, if-else branches, and loop bodies all use {}. Mismatched or missing braces cause compilation errors.

question: Which operator concatenates two String values in Java?
feedback: The + operator joins strings: "Hello, " + "World" produces "Hello, World". When one operand is a String, Java converts the other side to text automatically: "Score: " + 42 produces "Score: 42".

question: What does the return keyword do inside a method?
correct: Exits the method and optionally passes a value back to the caller
incorrect: Prints a value to the console
Pauses the method until a condition is true
Declares what type of value the method will produce
feedback: return ends the current method. For a non-void method, 'return value;' provides the result to the caller. For void methods, 'return;' (with no value) exits early. The return type in the method signature declares what kind of value will be returned.

question: What does a variable provide in a Java program?
correct: A named location in memory that holds a value which can change during execution
incorrect: A permanent, unchangeable value used throughout the program
The name of a method to call later
A reference to an external file
feedback: Variables are named storage locations. Their values can change as the program runs (unless declared final). Variables let programs work with data that is unknown at compile time, such as user input or computed results.

question: Which of the following claims about Java's 'Write Once, Run Anywhere' model are correct?
correct: javac compiles Java source code into platform-independent bytecode
The bytecode is executed by the Java Virtual Machine, not directly by the OS
The same .class file can run on any platform that has a compatible JVM installed
incorrect: Java source files are executed directly without compilation
Each target platform requires its own separately compiled version of the program
feedback: Java achieves portability in two steps: javac compiles source to bytecode (.class files), then each platform's JVM interprets that bytecode. The .class file is platform-independent; only the JVM is platform-specific.

question: Which of the following are valid Java variable declarations?
correct: int score = 100;
String name = "Alice";
boolean isReady = false;
incorrect: int number = "42";
number = 5;
feedback: Each declaration must specify a type, a name, and a compatible value. int number = "42" is a type mismatch (String cannot go into an int). number = 5 omits the type — valid as an assignment to an already-declared variable, but not a declaration.

question: Which of the following are true about the while loop in Java?
correct: The condition is evaluated before each iteration
If the condition is false on the first check, the loop body never executes
While loops are well-suited for situations where the number of iterations is not known in advance
incorrect: The loop body always runs at least once
While loops run faster than equivalent for loops
feedback: A while loop pre-checks its condition; if it starts false, the body never runs. For loops are preferred when the count is known; while loops suit 'keep going until something happens' patterns. A do-while loop (not discussed here) guarantees at least one execution.

question: Which of the following are true about Java comparison operators?
correct: == tests whether two primitive values are equal
!= tests whether two values are not equal
Comparison expressions evaluate to a boolean result
incorrect: = is the operator for testing equality in a condition
== reliably compares the contents of two String objects
feedback: == and != return boolean values. Using = in a condition is an assignment, not a comparison. For String equality, use .equals() — == on Strings compares memory addresses, not content.

question: Which of the following are correct statements about void methods?
correct: A void method does not return any value to its caller
A void method can contain 'return;' to exit early
void methods are appropriate when the goal is to perform an action, not compute a result
incorrect: A void method cannot call other methods
Using a void method call where a value is expected is a runtime error
feedback: void means no return value. void methods can use 'return;' to exit early and can freely call other methods. Using a void call where a value is expected (e.g., int x = myVoidMethod()) is a compile-time error, not a runtime error.

question: Which of the following are true about the for loop in Java?
correct: A for loop has three parts: initialization, condition, and update
The update expression runs after each iteration
A for loop is well-suited when the number of iterations is known in advance
incorrect: The initialization expression runs again at the start of every iteration
i++ in the update runs before the loop body executes
feedback: In a for loop, the initialization runs once before the loop starts; the condition is checked before each iteration; the update runs after each iteration. i++ is post-increment — the body uses the current value, then i is incremented for the next cycle.

question: Which of the following are valid logical operators in Java?
incorrect: AND
OR
NOT
feedback: && (AND), || (OR), and ! (NOT) are Java's logical operators. AND, OR, and NOT are English words or operators from other languages — they are not valid Java syntax.

question: The file extension for a human-readable Java source file is ___.
correct: .java
feedback: Java source files end in .java. After compiling with javac, the output is a .class file containing platform-independent bytecode.

question: The Java class used to read input from the keyboard is ___.
correct: Scanner
feedback: Scanner is in the java.util package. Import it with 'import java.util.Scanner;' then create: Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);

question: In Java, the expression 7 / 2 evaluates to ___ when both values are of type int.
feedback: Integer division truncates the decimal: 7 / 2 = 3, not 3.5. To get a decimal, at least one operand must be a double: 7.0 / 2 = 3.5.

question: A single-line comment in Java begins with ___.
feedback: // starts a single-line comment. The compiler ignores everything from // to the end of the line. Multi-line comments use /* ... */.

question: To make a JFrame window visible, you call setVisible(___).
correct: true
feedback: JFrame objects start hidden. Calling setVisible(true) displays the window on screen. Forgetting this is a common mistake — the program compiles and runs, but no window appears.

question: The keyword that sends a value back from a method to its caller is ___.
correct: return
feedback: return exits the method and passes a value back to wherever the method was called. For void methods, 'return;' (no value) exits early but is optional at the end of a method.

question: Match each Java data type to the kind of value it stores.

pairs.definition: A whole number with no decimal point, such as 42 or -7
A decimal floating-point number, such as 3.14 or -0.5
One of exactly two values: true or false
A sequence of characters enclosed in double quotes, such as "Hello"

feedback: int and double are numeric primitives. boolean is Java's logical type — it only accepts true or false. String is a class (not a primitive) but is the standard way to work with text.

question: Match each Java Swing component to its primary role.

pairs.definition: The top-level window container for a desktop application
Displays non-interactive text or an image in the window
A clickable button the user can interact with
An interface whose method is called when a user event occurs

feedback: JFrame is the outermost container. JLabel and JButton are added to it. Registering an ActionListener on a JButton tells Java which code to run when the button is clicked.

question: Match each Java comparison operator to what it tests.

pairs.definition: Tests whether two values are equal
Tests whether two values are not equal
Tests whether the left value is less than the right
Tests whether the left value is greater than the right

feedback: All comparison operators return a boolean. Do not confuse = (assignment) with == (equality test). For String content comparison, use .equals() instead of ==.

question: Match each Java file type to its role in the build-and-run process.

pairs.definition: Human-readable source code written by the programmer
Platform-independent bytecode produced by the javac compiler
An archive packaging .class files for distribution or execution

feedback: Write .java source → compile with javac → run .class with java. A .jar bundles .class files and can be run with 'java -jar' or used as a library dependency.

question: Match each logical operator to its behavior.

pairs.definition: True only when both operands are true
True when at least one operand is true
Reverses the boolean value of its single operand

feedback: Java uses short-circuit evaluation: for &&, if the left side is false the right side is skipped; for ||, if the left side is true the right side is skipped. This avoids unnecessary work and prevents side-effect bugs.

question: Match each loop type to the scenario where it fits most naturally.

pairs.definition: Repeating a known number of times with an explicit counter
Repeating until a condition changes, when the count is not known in advance

feedback: Use for when you know the iteration count in advance (e.g., loop 10 times). Use while when you repeat until an event occurs (e.g., keep reading until the user types 'quit'). Either can technically handle either case, but the right choice makes code more readable.
